title: Survival phrases
subtitle: Please, sorry, and what to say when language fails

vocabulary.exampleMeaning: Excuse me, where is the train station?

hook.text: The most important thing you can learn in a language isn't how to perform perfectly — it's how to recover gracefully when you don't understand. These phrases keep the conversation going when your Russian runs out. A learner who says 'повторите, пожалуйста' is far more impressive than one who just goes silent.

why.text: Survival phrases serve two functions: they get you what you need in real situations, and they signal to native speakers that you're making a genuine effort. Russians tend to be very encouraging to language learners who clearly try. Saying пожалуйста and извините correctly opens more doors than a perfect grammar sentence delivered coldly.

cards.title: Please and you're welcome — пожалуйста
cards.body: пожалуйста does double duty: it means 'please' when making a request AND 'you're welcome' when someone thanks you. It's placed after the request or standalone as a polite response. Pronunciation: the Л is soft and the whole word flows — pah-ZHAH-luh-stah.

cards.title: Two ways to say sorry
cards.body: извините is formal — use it to get someone's attention, apologise to a stranger, or in professional settings. прости is informal — use it with friends when you've made a small mistake. The same formal/informal split as здравствуйте/привет.

examples.meaning: No problem / it's okay (response to an apology)

cards.title: When language fails
cards.body: These three phrases are your emergency kit. Use them immediately and without embarrassment — they're far better than nodding and pretending to understand.

cards.title: Negation — не
cards.body: не (ne) is the negation particle. Place it directly before the word you're negating — almost always before the verb. Я не понимаю = I don't understand (not: Я понимаю не). не never changes form.

questions.prompt: Someone thanks you — спасибо! What do you say?
questions.explanation: пожалуйста = you're welcome. It also means 'please' — it covers both.

questions.prompt: You don't understand what someone said. What do you say?
questions.explanation: Я не понимаю. (Ya ne ponimayu.) = I don't understand.

questions.prompt: What does не do in a sentence?
questions.explanation: не is the negation particle — place it before the verb or word you're negating.

cards.title: где — the most useful single word
cards.body: где (gde) = where. A single word question with где + noun will get you very far: Где туалет? (Where is the toilet?) Где метро? (Where is the metro?) Где вокзал? (Where is the train station?) No verb needed — Russians understand immediately.

cards.title: Я не знаю — I don't know
cards.body: Я не знаю (ya ne znayu) is another essential phrase. You'll use it constantly. Notice the pattern: я (I) + не (not) + verb. This is how almost all negation in first person works. The verb знать (to know) conjugates to знаю for я.

questions.explanation: пожалуйста covers both 'please' (making a request) and 'you're welcome' (responding to thanks).

questions.prompt: How do you say 'where is the bank?' in Russian?
questions.explanation: Где банк? (Gde bank?) = Where is the bank? где = where; банк = bank.
Повторите, пожалуйста. (Povtorite, pozhaluysta.) = Please repeat that.

questions.prompt: What is the formal way to say 'excuse me'?
questions.explanation: извините (izvinite) = excuse me / sorry (formal). Informal version: прости.

questions.prompt: Type the Russian for 'I don't understand':
questions.explanation: Я не понимаю. (Ya ne ponimayu.) = I don't understand.
не is placed before the word you're negating — always before the verb in basic sentences.
говорите медленно (govoritye medlenno) = speak slowly. говорите = speak (formal imperative), медленно = slowly.

rules: пожалуйста = please (requests) AND you're welcome (after thanks)
извините = excuse me / sorry (formal); прости = sorry (informal)
Survival kit: Я не понимаю / Повторите, пожалуйста / Говорите медленно, пожалуйста
где + noun = 'where is X?' — no verb needed
не before the verb = negation: Я не знаю = I don't know
summary.nextLesson: Next block — Core grammar. First up: noun gender and plurals — why стол becomes столы and книга becomes книги.

questions.prompt: Type the Russian for 'I don't understand':
Which is the formal word for 'excuse me'?
How do you ask 'where is the table?' in Russian?
What does не do in a sentence?
